document.title: Zoo Modernization Design Note
document.purpose: Capture the design bridge between the 2022 Zoo implementation and the newer Zoo storage and indexing model.
author: Lion Kimbro
ChatGPT

overview.basic: Zoo is being modernized from an older GUI application built around a single registry file and project directories into a filesystem-based system where each project has its own JSON file and the runtime operates primarily from an index.
overview.goal: Preserve the strengths of the earlier Zoo application while replacing the storage model with a simpler, faster, more inspectable architecture.
source-material: Lion's Python Programming Guidelines (2025) -- Simplified Edition

preserve: Preserve the interaction model of a fast search window leading into recurring per-project windows.
Preserve the small core project vocabulary: id, tag, title, created, tags, description, hook.
Preserve the filesystem-first philosophy: project files are authoritative and indexes are derived caches.
Preserve the direct procedural implementation style: explicit functions, simple globals, simple JSON I/O, minimal abstraction.
discard-or-translate: Discard the single authoritative registry file model used by the 2022 application.
Discard the assumption that every project must have a dedicated on-disk folder.
Discard the old LOC field as a core part of project identity or required storage behavior.
Translate load-all-projects behavior into index-based browsing plus per-project file reads when a specific project is opened.

root-resolution.meaning: Zoo storage is rooted at the path returned by Machine-Root for db-root-2026.

project-files.authority: Each project file is authoritative for that project.
project-files.shape: Each project is stored as a separate JSON file under projects/.
project-files.note: A project may exist with no additional on-disk material besides its project JSON file and corresponding index entry.

index.role: The index is the fast runtime structure used for enumeration, search, and quick UI startup.
index.authority: The index is derived and not authoritative.

runtime-behavior: On startup, the application should read index.json and keep it in memory for normal browsing and search.
When the user opens a specific project, the application may then read that project's JSON file.
Before any write to index.json, the application should reread index.json from disk, apply the amendment to that fresh copy, and then write it back.
A rebuild path should exist so that index.json can be regenerated from the project files.
ui-implications: Search and result listing should operate from the in-memory index rather than by scanning every project file.
The hook field belongs in the index so that result lists can show the hook without opening each project file.
Per-project windows should load full project data only when needed.
implementation-direction: Replace hard-coded legacy paths with Machine-Root based resolution.
Replace the old registry-backed project access layer with an index-backed browsing layer and per-project file access layer.
Keep the implementation procedurally simple and readable rather than introducing heavy frameworks or abstractions.
notes: The 2022 Zoo code should be treated as an interaction and implementation ancestor, not as the storage model to preserve literally.
Fast summon and dismiss behavior is a primary design goal for the modernized application.
